greeting_style: Bow instead of shaking hands. The depth of the bow reflects the level of respect — a slight nod for casual acquaintance, a 45-degree bow for formal situations. Exchanging business cards is ceremonial: present and receive with both hands, take time to read it, never write on it or put it in your back pocket.
dining_etiquette: Wait to be seated. Say 'itadakimasu' before eating and 'gochisousama' after. Do not stick chopsticks upright in rice (funeral omen). Do not pass food chopstick-to-chopstick. Slurping noodles loudly is acceptable and signals enjoyment. Eating while walking is frowned upon.
dress_considerations: Conservative but fashion-forward in cities. Remove shoes before entering homes and many traditional restaurants (you'll see the entryway and shoe rack). Cover up at temples and shrines — scarves are useful.
major_taboos: Tipping is considered rude and can cause offense — it implies the server needs charity. Pointing directly at people is rude; use an open hand. Avoid blowing your nose in public. Public displays of affection are uncommon.
tipping_culture: No tipping. Outstanding service is the baseline expectation, not something to be rewarded additionally. Attempting to tip may cause confusion or be politely refused.

greeting_style: Greet with 'Namaste' (palms pressed together, slight bow) — universally appropriate across regions. Handshakes are common in business settings, especially in cities. Traditional Hindu and Muslim customs mean women may not shake hands with men from different communities; let them initiate.
dining_etiquette: Eat with your right hand only (left is considered unclean). In traditional settings, food is eaten from a banana leaf or thali. Guests are expected to eat generously — refusing food can be seen as rude. It is polite to compliment the cook. Do not eat beef in Hindu households; no pork in Muslim ones.
dress_considerations: Dress modestly outside major cities and tourist areas. Cover shoulders and knees when visiting temples, mosques, and rural areas. Remove shoes at temples. Socks are not required but keep feet clean. Bright colors are celebrated.
major_taboos: Never touch someone's head (considered sacred). Avoid pointing feet at people or religious objects (feet are considered the lowest, most impure part of the body). Do not touch sacred objects without permission. Public displays of affection are inappropriate outside tourist areas.
tipping_culture: Tipping is customary and expected: 10% in restaurants, small amounts for hotel staff and drivers. Bargaining is expected at markets but not at fixed-price shops. Always confirm prices in advance.

greeting_style: Warm and expressive. Italians kiss on both cheeks (left first) between friends and acquaintances. Handshakes in professional settings. Using first names quickly is common; Italians are not formal.
dining_etiquette: Lunch is the main meal and can last 2-3 hours. Dinner typically starts at 8 PM or later. Do not ask for cheese on seafood pasta (considered a culinary crime). Cappuccino is a morning drink only — ordering one after noon marks you as a tourist. Bread is for eating between courses, not for dipping in olive oil (that's a tourist invention). The bill will not come until you ask.
dress_considerations: Smart casual is appropriate in cities. Italians are well-dressed and notice when others are not. Cover shoulders and knees in churches — a scarf in your bag is useful. Swimwear belongs at the beach, not on city streets.
major_taboos: Cutting pasta with a knife is offensive to Italian sensibilities. Do not clap between movements at classical music performances. Do not rush a meal. Arriving too early to a dinner party (more than 15 minutes before stated time) is considered rude.
tipping_culture: Not required but appreciated. Rounding up the bill or leaving a few euros is standard. 10-15% for exceptional service in tourist areas. Service charge (coperto or servizio) is often already included — check the bill.

greeting_style: The wai — pressing palms together at chest level with a slight bow — is the standard greeting. The height of your hands reflects the level of respect. Return a wai when you receive one. Smiling is extremely important; Thailand is called the Land of Smiles. Monks should not be touched by women.
dining_etiquette: Communal dishes are shared from the center of the table. It is polite to serve others before yourself. Use a spoon rather than a fork to eat rice dishes. Chopsticks are mainly for noodle soups. Leave a little food on your plate to signal you have had enough — finishing everything suggests you are still hungry.
dress_considerations: Modest dress is required at temples: covered shoulders, elbows, and knees. Many temples provide sarong wraps at the entrance. Shoes are removed before entering temples and many homes. Beach cover-ups are expected when leaving beach areas.
major_taboos: The monarchy is deeply revered — never speak disrespectfully about the King or royal family (this is a criminal offense under lèse-majesté law). Never touch someone's head, even affectionately. Never point your feet at people or toward Buddha images. Losing your temper publicly causes significant loss of face for everyone involved.
tipping_culture: Tipping is appreciated but not mandatory. Round up taxi fares. 20-50 baht for hotel staff. 10% in restaurants that do not include service. Massage therapists: 50-100 baht tip.
key_phrase: Khop khun krap/ka (thank you, krap for men, ka for women)

greeting_style: Firm handshakes are standard in all settings. Germans value punctuality — arriving late is considered disrespectful. First names are used only after being invited; use Herr (Mr.) or Frau (Ms./Mrs.) with surname until then. Germans tend toward directness; do not mistake a lack of small talk for rudeness.
dining_etiquette: Germans eat with fork in left hand and knife in right. Do not put hands in your lap during meals (both hands should be visible at the table, resting on wrists). Finishing everything on your plate is considered polite and complimentary to the cook. In beer halls, it is normal to sit with strangers at long communal tables.
dress_considerations: Smart casual is the norm. Germans dress practically and do not typically overdress for casual settings, but expect business settings to be formal. FKK (Freikörperkultur — nude bathing) is widely practiced and accepted at designated areas — this is not considered provocative.
major_taboos: Refusing to make eye contact during a toast is considered rude (and, per German superstition, brings bad luck). Do not jaywalk — Germans take traffic laws seriously and will comment. Do not confuse Austrian or Swiss culture with German. Never make Nazi-related jokes or gestures — this is socially unacceptable and in some cases illegal.
tipping_culture: Tipping 10% is the standard practice. Round up for taxi rides. Tip is given directly to the server in cash, not left on the table when you leave.

greeting_style: Brazilians are warm, tactile, and close-contact communicators. Air kisses on both cheeks (one kiss in São Paulo, two in Rio and other cities) for women and mixed-gender greetings. Men shake hands or hug. Personal space is smaller than Northern European or North American norms.
dining_etiquette: Brazilians eat a large lunch and lighter dinner. Feijoada (black bean stew) is traditionally served on Wednesdays and Saturdays. Brazilian barbecue (churrascaria) is social and extended. It is rude to leave immediately after eating — socializing before and after is expected. The meal ends when the host signals.
dress_considerations: Beaches are for bikinis and board shorts — Brazilians wear very little at the beach and this is completely normal. Cities are more conservative. Smart casual is appropriate in restaurants and business settings. Dress well for nightlife — Brazilians take appearance seriously when going out.
major_taboos: Political discussions can become heated — avoid unless you know your audience well. Do not make the okay gesture (circle with thumb and forefinger) — in Brazil, this is an obscene gesture. Do not assume all Brazilians speak Spanish (the language is Portuguese). Pointing with one finger is rude; use an open hand.
tipping_culture: Tipping 10% is common and usually included in the bill as a service charge (garçom). If included, you are not obligated to add more. Taxi drivers do not typically expect a tip, but rounding up is appreciated.

greeting_style: Bow as a greeting — the depth reflects respect level. Hierarchical culture means seniority matters greatly: use proper honorifics, pour drinks for elders before yourself, and let elders sit and begin eating first. Two-handed giving and receiving (of objects, business cards, cups) shows respect.
dining_etiquette: Wait for the eldest to begin eating before you start. Do not finish your food completely (it may signal you were not given enough). Soup and rice should not be lifted from the table — eat from the bowl while it rests on the table. Pouring your own drink is impolite; wait for others to pour for you, and pour for others proactively. Sharing dishes is standard; individual portions are uncommon in traditional settings.
dress_considerations: Modern and fashionable in cities. Koreans are very style-conscious, particularly in Seoul. Cover up at temples and traditional settings. Age and status are often reflected in dress — older, more conservative attire in formal or elder company.
major_taboos: Age hierarchy is important — asking someone's age upon meeting is common and not rude (it determines how to speak to them). Never refuse food from an elder without a very polite excuse. Splitting a bill is uncommon — usually one person pays, and reciprocity is expected over time.
tipping_culture: Tipping is not customary and can cause awkwardness. Service is simply expected as part of the job. The exception is some upscale hotels catering to international visitors.

greeting_style: Warm and expressive. Handshakes for initial greetings, progressing to one-cheek kisses as relationships develop. Formality matters: use usted (formal 'you') with elders and strangers until invited to use tú. Family is central to Mexican culture — ask about family to show genuine interest.
dining_etiquette: Lunch (comida) is the largest meal, typically from 2-4 PM. Dinner is light and late. Tasting food before adding salt or hot sauce is polite (adding immediately implies it is not seasoned well). It is common for the host to insist you eat more; gentle refusal and then accepting a small second helping is polite. Street food is a legitimate and important part of Mexican food culture.
dress_considerations: Casual in everyday settings. Smart casual for restaurants and social gatherings. Beach and resort attire stays at the beach. Dress more conservatively when visiting churches or smaller traditional towns.
major_taboos: Do not refer to the United States as 'America' — Mexico is also part of America. Avoid discussing the drug trade or cartels casually. Do not photograph religious ceremonies or indigenous people without permission. Bargaining is appropriate at markets, not at restaurants or established stores.
tipping_culture: 10-15% in restaurants is standard. Peso coins for parking attendants and gas station helpers. Round up for taxi drivers. Hotel staff 20-50 pesos per day. Tipping is an important part of service workers' income.
